name: OWP Productivity Insights
source: cortex-mockup repo · 53 cortex output JSONs + 17 supabase rollups · 65 unique OWP jobs

usage_instructions.purpose: This file is the productivity-calibration layer for the OWP bidding tool. It supplements Cortex's comparable-matching by providing cost-code-level historical data so the bid output reflects how OWP actually executes, not just how it estimates.
usage_instructions.consumed_by: Claude or any agent generating an OWP bid via the bidding tool

scope_of_responsibility.this_file: Carries productivity-driven calibration data (per-code rates, variances, structural rules) and instructions for applying it.
scope_of_responsibility.cortex: Handles comparable-job selection by size, GC, project type, vintage, etc. This file does NOT segment by cohort — Cortex does.
scope_of_responsibility.estimator: Produces the takeoff (units, fixtures, code-level quantities). This file calibrates that takeoff.

required_inputs_from_caller: estimator_takeoff (dict by cost code) — the bid's draft line items
comparable_job_ids (list of strings) — provided by Cortex; the IDs whose history this file should pull from

per_code_aggregate_benchmarks.use_when: comparable_job_ids returned <5 jobs OR code is missing from comparables; full-dataset fallback

per_job_per_code_history.path: (external) cost_code_long table — one row per (job, code)
per_job_per_code_history.use_when: Cortex returns ≥5 comparable jobs; aggregate THESE jobs' rows for the calibration

macro_benchmarks.use_when: Sanity-checking the bid total at the project level

structural_rules.use_when: Always — these are the OWP-BID-### policy rules that override default math

workflow.name: Comparable selection
workflow.description: Cortex returns N comparable past jobs based on size, GC, project type, etc. Hand the list of job IDs forward to step 2.

workflow.name: Retrieve per-code productivity from comparables
workflow.description: For each comparable job ID, pull its (job, code) rows from the long table. Aggregate by code: median(hours_per_unit), median(actual_per_unit), median(variance_orig_to_actual).
workflow.fallback: If the comparable set has <5 jobs for a particular code, supplement with cost_code_benchmarks.by_code[code] (full-dataset benchmark).

workflow.name: Compute calibrated rates per code
workflow.description: For each code in the takeoff: expected_hours = units × median(hours_per_unit); expected_dollars = units × median(actual_per_unit); tuning_factor = 1 + median(variance_orig_to_actual); calibrated_value = estimator_takeoff × tuning_factor.

workflow.name: Apply structural adjustments
workflow.description: These overrides apply on top of the calibrated rates from step 3. They encode known systematic errors in OWP's takeoff process.

adjustments.rationale: Only major labor code that systematically OVERRUNS estimates (median +10% across 50 jobs). Material cushion does not protect labor exposure.

adjustments.adjustment: Choose cushion policy explicitly: (a) tighten takeoff by ~24%, OR (b) hold cushion as declared margin contribution. Do not let cushion compound implicitly.
adjustments.rationale: Material codes systematically come in under original budget (median −24% across all material; individual codes range −14% to −47%).
Median +106% variance, but on small dollars (~$4.6k median actual). Indicates takeoff convention issue, not a bid-tuning lever.

workflow.name: Price labor at fully-loaded wage
workflow.description: Convert hours to dollars at fully-loaded wage. Default $40.23/hr (median); use current loaded wage if known.
workflow.warning: Do NOT use blended wage alone — burden multiplier is 1.34× median; understatement of true labor cost otherwise.

workflow.name: Macro sanity-check
workflow.description: Validate bid totals against project-level benchmarks. Flag for review if any check fails.

workflow.name: Document the tuning
workflow.description: Bid output must include enough audit trail for an analyst to retrace the math.
required_in_output: List of comparable job IDs used (from Cortex)
Per line item: estimator_takeoff, calibrated_value, rate source (comparable subset vs full-dataset fallback), n_jobs behind that rate
Macro sanity-check results — pass/flag per metric
Any flags raised that need human decision

calibration_math.expected_hours_per_code: units × median(hours_per_unit from comparables for that code)
calibration_math.expected_dollars_per_code: units × median(actual_per_unit from comparables for that code)
calibration_math.tuning_factor_per_code: 1 + median(variance_orig_to_actual from comparables for that code)
calibration_math.interpretation_of_variance: Negative = OWP comes in UNDER original budget on average. Positive = overruns. Tuning factor < 1 means apply a cushion; > 1 means apply a correction.

fallback_rules: Comparables <5 for a code → use cost_code_benchmarks.by_code[code] full-dataset benchmark.
Comparables = 0 (no Cortex match) OR Cortex unavailable → use full-dataset benchmarks throughout; flag the bid as 'uncalibrated to comparables'.
Code n_jobs_with_data < 20 in full dataset → do NOT apply variance correction; use estimator's takeoff verbatim and note 'low historical N'.
Code missing from cost_code_benchmarks entirely → use estimator's takeoff verbatim; flag as 'unknown code'.
Live jobs (is_live=true) → DO NOT use as comparables for calibration. Their data is partial.

quality_thresholds.single_comparable: Do not auto-tune. Flag for analyst review and present the comparable's data alongside full-dataset benchmark.
quality_thresholds.wide_variance_band: If P75 − P25 > 0.30 for a code, present both endpoints; recommend the user choose the tighter or looser end deliberately rather than picking the midpoint silently.
quality_thresholds.out_of_range_units: If new project's plumbing_units is outside historical 18–398 range, flag — extrapolation risk.
quality_thresholds.missing_gc_match: If Cortex returned comparables based on size/type only (no GC match), note that the calibration is GC-agnostic and may miss GC-specific behavior.

what_this_file_does_not_provide: Cohort-segmented benchmarks (size/GC/vintage breakouts) — Cortex computes these on the fly from the per-job index.
Real-time wage / burden updates — defaults are historical medians; pass current values in the bid request to override.
Subcontractor pricing — code 600 is flagged for human review.

data_freshness.refresh_trigger: Re-extract when new completed jobs are added to the cortex-mockup repo OR when material/labor variance medians shift by >5 percentage points across the most recent 10 jobs.

usage_instructions.version_notes: v1.2 — adds usage_instructions block per OWP request; cost_code_benchmarks unchanged from v1.1.

headline_findings.title: OWP material takeoffs systematically come in under original budget
headline_findings.summary: Across 50 completed jobs, the median material variance from original budget to actual spend is -23.9%. Negative means UNDER budget. This is the strongest single signal in the dataset for calibrating the bid model.
headline_findings.implication: Two valid responses for the bid model: (a) tighten the takeoff factor by ~25% and bid a leaner number, or (b) hold the cushion as a declared margin contribution and report it explicitly to leadership. Without an explicit policy, this cushion compounds opaquely.

headline_findings.title: Rough-in labor (code 120) is the ONLY major labor code that runs over budget
headline_findings.summary: Across 50 completed jobs, code 120 (rough-in labor) has a median original→actual variance of +10.2%. Every other major labor code (supervision 100, takeoff 101, underground 110, trim 130, water main 141, mech room 142) runs UNDER budget. This means takeoff hours for rough-in are systematically too low.
headline_findings.implication: Bid model should apply a +10–15% correction factor to rough-in labor takeoff specifically. Material cushion alone does not compensate for this — labor is harder to recover once underbid. Validate against estimator who owns the 120 takeoff: is the unit rate stale, or is scope creep happening between takeoff and field?

headline_findings.title: House standard for labor productivity is ~123 hours per plumbing unit
headline_findings.summary: Median is 123 hrs/unit (P25 94, P75 154). The middle 50% of jobs sit between 94 and 154 hrs/unit — that is the credible band for a like-for-like new bid.
headline_findings.implication: Bids that fall below P25 or above P75 should be flagged for explicit justification — the bidding tool should not silently extrapolate beyond the historical band.

headline_findings.title: Gross margin clusters at 41.4%
headline_findings.summary: Median gross margin is 41.4%. This is what OWP earns once direct cost (labor + material + burden + tax) is removed from billed revenue. The bid model should default to this margin as the floor; anything materially below P25 is unacceptable.
headline_findings.implication: Use as default target margin. Combine with material-takeoff cushion finding for total margin posture.

headline_findings.title: Fully loaded labor cost is ~$40.23/hr (1.34× blended)
headline_findings.summary: Median fully loaded wage (labor + burden) is $40.23/hr. Burden multiplier of 1.34× means OWP carries ~34% on top of direct wages for taxes, insurance, vehicles, etc. Bid models that price labor at blended wage understate true cost by this factor.
headline_findings.implication: Always price labor at fully-loaded wage in the bid model.

headline_findings.title: Labor variance is roughly balanced (-5.0% median)
headline_findings.summary: Unlike material (consistently under), labor variance from original budget to actual is closer to neutral (median -5.0%). The bid model should NOT assume a labor cushion the way it can for material.
headline_findings.implication: Bid the labor number close to the takeoff — there is no systematic safety margin.

headline_findings.title: Typical crew is 5.0 FTE-equivalent
headline_findings.summary: Median average crew FTE is 5.0. Useful sanity check for scheduling: a 123-hr/unit job with a typical crew implies ~14.2 months for 100 units.
headline_findings.implication: Use for duration sanity-checks and resource planning.

overtime_pct_of_hours.note: OT cost code hours / total hours

top5_hour_concentration.note: Top-5 workers as fraction of total hours

material_variance_orig_to_actual.note: Negative = under original budget — the strongest single calibration signal

bid_calibration_rules.rationale: Median across 50 completed jobs with unit counts.
bid_calibration_rules.applies_when: Plumbing unit count is known. For non-residential jobs without unit counts, fall back to historical labor%revenue.

bid_calibration_rules.statement: Price labor at fully-loaded wage of $40.23/hr (or pass-through of current loaded wage)
bid_calibration_rules.rationale: Burden multiplier of 1.34× consistently observed. Blended wage alone understates true cost.

bid_calibration_rules.statement: Apply ~24% takeoff cushion / margin contribution on material
bid_calibration_rules.rationale: Median material original→actual variance is -23.9%. Either tighten takeoff or hold as explicit margin. Communicate the choice to leadership rather than burying it.
bid_calibration_rules.applies_when: Material takeoff is being generated. Re-evaluate per project type — variance is wider on smaller jobs.
Always. Bids below P25 (33.9%) require sign-off.

bid_calibration_rules.rationale: Crew FTE median is 5.0; throughput derived from hours/month median 868.
bid_calibration_rules.applies_when: After labor estimate is set, validate duration is feasible given typical crew capacity.

bid_calibration_rules.statement: Carry overhead burden of ~34% on top of direct labor (cost code 995 equivalent)
bid_calibration_rules.rationale: Burden ratio embedded in fully loaded wage; if labor priced at blended wage, this needs to be added separately.
bid_calibration_rules.applies_when: Whenever labor is itemized at blended wage instead of loaded wage.

bid_calibration_rules.statement: Apply a +12% correction to rough-in labor (code 120) takeoff
bid_calibration_rules.rationale: Code 120 median variance is +10.2% across 50 jobs — it is the only major labor code that systematically overruns. Material cushion does not cover labor exposure.
bid_calibration_rules.applies_when: Always, until estimator-side fix is implemented.

jobs_excluded_from_benchmarks.note: Live and low-hour jobs are kept in Per-Job Rollup but excluded from medians.

data_gaps.note: Per-unit metrics skipped where units missing. Cost-share metrics skipped where revenue missing.

schema_caveats.V_TX: Transaction-only schema (jobs 2111, 2112). Phase splits inferred from cost code numbers, not native fields.
schema_caveats.SUPABASE: Pre-rolled benchmark format. Worker-level wage data not present; vendor data not present.
schema_caveats.V1: Original cortex output. Codes/workers as dicts.
schema_caveats.V2.2: Newer cortex output. Codes/workers as lists with derived_fields and line_items.

known_anomalies: Job 2020 cortex output was empty; supabase rollup used as fallback.
Some material variance values exceed -50% (heavy under-budget) — likely conservative early-phase takeoffs on residential repeat jobs. Excluded outliers do not change median materially.
Wage percentiles only computed where worker-level hours/wage data exists (V1 and V2.2 schemas).

cost_code_benchmarks.methodology: For each cost code that appears across completed jobs (≥1,500 hrs total), aggregate hours, actual spend, original budget, and original→actual variance. P25/median/P75 across jobs. Applied to 1,510 (job, code) records spanning 63 jobs and 47 unique codes.
cost_code_benchmarks.note_low_coverage: Codes with fewer than 20 jobs of data are present in by_code for completeness but should not anchor bid defaults. Calibration signals below filter to ≥20 jobs.
